title: 02 — Linear Regression & Regularization
body:
**Linear regression** fits $\hat{y} = w^Tx + b$ by minimizing the sum of squared residuals (least squares): $J(w) = \sum_i (y_i - w^Tx_i)^2$. In closed form, $w^* = (X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty$ (the normal equations) — this requires $X^TX$ to be invertible, which fails when features are collinear or when $p > n$ (more features than samples). Gradient descent is the alternative when the closed form is too expensive (large $p$) or $X^TX$ is singular/ill-conditioned.

**Regularization** adds a penalty on the weights to control model complexity and fight overfitting/collinearity:
- **Ridge (L2)**: $J(w) + \lambda \|w\|_2^2$. Shrinks all weights smoothly toward zero but never exactly to zero; has a closed form $w^* = (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty$, which is always invertible for $\lambda > 0$ — this is the practical fix for collinear features.
- **Lasso (L1)**: $J(w) + \lambda \|w\|_1$. Drives some weights exactly to zero — performs **feature selection** as a side effect. No closed form (the L1 penalty isn't differentiable at 0); solved with coordinate descent or subgradient methods.
- **Elastic Net** mixes both penalties to get sparsity with more stable selection among correlated features than pure Lasso.

**Bias–variance angle.** Increasing $\lambda$ increases bias but decreases variance — it's a direct knob on the bias-variance trade-off (see the Model Selection section). $\lambda$ is chosen via cross-validation, never by looking at test-set performance.

**Exam angle.** Be ready to (a) derive/state the normal equations and explain when they fail, (b) explain *why* L1 induces sparsity geometrically (the L1 ball has corners on the axes where the loss contours are likely to first touch) while L2's ball is smooth, and (c) reason about what happens to weights and bias/variance as $\lambda \to 0$ or $\lambda \to \infty$.

title: 04 — Model Selection & Bias-Variance
body:
**Bias–variance decomposition.** Expected test error decomposes as $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible noise}$. **Bias** is the error from wrong modeling assumptions (an overly simple model that can't capture the true relationship — underfitting). **Variance** is the error from sensitivity to the particular training sample (an overly flexible model that fits noise — overfitting). Model complexity trades one for the other: simple models (low-degree polynomials, small $k$ in kNN, heavily regularized linear models) have high bias/low variance; complex models (high-degree polynomials, deep trees, large $k$-NN with small $k$, unregularized models) have low bias/high variance.

**Diagnosing under/overfitting.** Plot training error and validation error vs. model complexity (or vs. training-set size — a **learning curve**): both errors high and close together → underfitting (high bias); training error low but validation error much higher → overfitting (high variance). The best model sits at the complexity that minimizes **validation** error, not training error (training error decreases monotonically with complexity and is therefore not a valid selection criterion by itself).

**Model selection procedure.** Never touch the test set during selection: split into train/validation(/test) or use k-fold cross-validation on the training set to choose hyperparameters (degree, $\lambda$, $k$, tree depth, kernel parameters, ...), then retrain on all training data with the chosen hyperparameters and evaluate once on the held-out test set for an unbiased estimate of generalization error.

**Exam angle.** Given a plot of train/validation error vs. complexity or vs. $\lambda$, identify whether the model is under/overfitting at a given point and say which direction to move the hyperparameter. Explain why using test-set performance to pick hyperparameters is methodologically wrong (it leaks test information into the model selection, so the final test score is no longer an unbiased estimate).

title: Feature Selection & PCA
body:
**Why reduce dimensionality.** High-dimensional feature spaces suffer the **curse of dimensionality** (data becomes sparse, distances become less meaningful, more parameters to estimate → higher variance) and redundant/correlated features add noise without adding signal.

**Feature selection** (keep a subset of the original features, so they stay interpretable):
- **Filter methods** — score each feature independently of the model (correlation with the target, mutual information, variance threshold) and keep the top-scoring ones; fast but ignores feature interactions.
- **Wrapper methods** — use the downstream model's performance to score subsets (forward selection: start empty, greedily add the feature that most improves validation performance; backward elimination: start with all, greedily remove the least useful); more accurate but expensive (retrains the model many times).
- **Embedded methods** — selection happens as part of training (Lasso's L1 penalty zeroing out coefficients, decision-tree feature importances).

**PCA (Principal Component Analysis)** instead *transforms* the features into new, uncorrelated dimensions (the *principal components*), ordered by the variance of the data they explain — it's a feature **extraction**/projection technique, not selection, so the resulting components are generally not directly interpretable in terms of the original features.
- Algorithm: center the data (subtract the mean), compute the covariance matrix, eigendecompose it — the eigenvectors are the principal components (directions of maximum variance) and the eigenvalues are the variance explained along each one. Project the data onto the top-$k$ eigenvectors to reduce to $k$ dimensions.
- Choosing $k$: pick enough components to retain a target fraction of total variance (e.g. 95%), read off a **scree plot** (eigenvalues sorted descending) for an "elbow."
- PCA is sensitive to feature scale — features must be standardized first, or high-variance-by-scale features will dominate the first components artificially.

**Exam angle.** Given a covariance matrix or a set of eigenvalues, compute the fraction of variance explained by the top-$k$ components. Explain why PCA components are ordered by eigenvalue and why standardization matters before applying PCA. Distinguish feature selection (keeps original, interpretable features) from PCA (creates new, uninterpretable, uncorrelated combinations).

title: 06 — Kernel Methods
body:
**Kernel trick.** When data isn't linearly separable in the original space, a **kernel function** $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T\phi(x_j)$ computes the dot product in some (possibly infinite-dimensional) transformed feature space $\phi$ *without ever computing $\phi$ explicitly* — many linear algorithms (SVM, ridge regression, PCA, ...) only ever need dot products between points, so any valid kernel can be substituted in wherever a dot product appears (the "kernel trick"), implicitly working in a much richer feature space at the cost of a single kernel evaluation instead of an explicit, possibly infinite-dimensional, feature mapping.

**Common kernels:** **linear** ($K = x_i^Tx_j$, equivalent to no transformation), **polynomial** ($K = (x_i^Tx_j + c)^d$, captures feature interactions up to degree $d$), **RBF/Gaussian** ($K = \exp(-\gamma\|x_i - x_j\|^2)$, corresponds to an infinite-dimensional feature space; $\gamma$ controls how far a single point's influence reaches — large $\gamma$ → narrow influence → higher variance/more complex boundary).

**Mercer's condition.** A function $K$ is a valid kernel (corresponds to a dot product in *some* feature space) iff its Gram matrix (the matrix of $K(x_i,x_j)$ over the dataset) is symmetric and positive semi-definite for any set of points — this is what lets you build/combine kernels (sums, products, scalings of valid kernels are still valid) without ever constructing $\phi$ by hand.

**Exam angle.** Given a small 2D dataset that isn't linearly separable, reason about which kernel (and which $\gamma$/$d$) would make it linearly separable in the transformed space. Explain *why* the kernel trick avoids computing $\phi(x)$ explicitly (only dot products are ever needed) and why that matters for infinite-dimensional feature spaces like the RBF kernel's.

title: 07 — Support Vector Machines (SVM)
body:
**Support Vector Machines (SVM)** find the linear separating hyperplane that **maximizes the margin** — the distance to the closest points of each class (the **support vectors**), which are the only points that determine the decision boundary (all other points could move or be removed without changing it). Maximizing the margin is the mechanism that gives SVMs good generalization even with relatively few examples.

**Hard-margin vs soft-margin.** Hard-margin SVM requires perfect linear separability. **Soft-margin SVM** introduces slack variables $\xi_i \ge 0$ allowing some margin violations/misclassifications, penalized by a regularization parameter $C$ in the objective $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum_i \xi_i$: large $C$ penalizes violations heavily (narrower margin, closer to hard-margin, higher variance/lower bias); small $C$ tolerates more violations (wider margin, more regularized, higher bias/lower variance) — $C$ plays the same complexity-control role as $\lambda$ in ridge regression, just inverted in direction.

**Kernelized SVM.** Substituting a kernel (see Kernel Methods) for the dot product in the SVM dual formulation lets SVM find a linear separator in the *transformed* feature space, which corresponds to a non-linear boundary in the original space — this is the most common use of the kernel trick and why SVM and kernels are usually taught back-to-back.

**Exam angle.** Explain why only support vectors matter for the decision boundary. Discuss the effect of increasing/decreasing $C$ (and, for a kernelized SVM, $\gamma$) on bias/variance and margin width — the SVM-specific instance of the general bias-variance trade-off.

title: Ensemble Methods
body:
**Why ensembles.** Combining multiple models can reduce variance (bagging), reduce bias (boosting), or both, provided the base models are reasonably accurate and their errors are not perfectly correlated (diversity is what makes averaging/voting help).

**Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating).** Train many instances of the same high-variance base learner (typically deep decision trees) independently, each on a different **bootstrap sample** (sampled with replacement from the training set), then average their predictions (regression) or majority-vote (classification). This reduces variance without increasing bias, because averaging many noisy-but-unbiased estimators cancels out uncorrelated noise. **Random Forests** add extra decorrelation on top of bagging: at each split, only a random subset of features is considered, further reducing correlation between trees and improving the variance-reduction effect.

**Boosting.** Trains **weak learners sequentially**, each new one focused on the mistakes of the ensemble so far — this primarily reduces **bias** (turns a collection of weak, high-bias learners into a strong one), at some risk of increased variance if run for too many rounds. **AdaBoost** reweights training examples after each round, upweighting misclassified points so the next weak learner focuses on them, and combines weak learners with weights based on their accuracy. **Gradient Boosting** instead fits each new learner to the *residual errors* (functional gradient of the loss) of the current ensemble.

**Bagging vs boosting, at a glance:** bagging trains learners independently/in parallel and mainly fights variance; boosting trains learners sequentially/dependently and mainly fights bias. Both work best when combined with a **base learner** that's simple (weak, for boosting) or high-variance (deep trees, for bagging).

**Exam angle.** Explain *why* bagging reduces variance (averaging i.i.d.-ish noisy estimators) but not bias, and why boosting reduces bias by design. Given a scenario, decide whether bagging or boosting is the better fit (bagging when the base model overfits; boosting when the base model underfits).

title: 03 — Classification (LogReg / Perceptron)
body:
**Logistic regression** models the probability of the positive class with the sigmoid of a linear score: $P(y=1|x) = \sigma(w^Tx+b) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-(w^Tx+b)}}$, and is trained by maximizing the likelihood of the observed labels, equivalently minimizing the **cross-entropy (log-loss)**: $J(w) = -\sum_i \big[y_i\log\hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\big]$. There's no closed form (unlike linear regression); it's optimized with gradient descent/Newton's method, and the loss is convex so gradient methods converge to the global optimum. Despite the name, it's a classifier: the decision boundary $P(y=1|x)=0.5$ is linear in $x$.

**Perceptron.** The classical linear classifier: predicts $\text{sign}(w^Tx + b)$ and updates weights only on misclassified points, $w \leftarrow w + \eta \, y_i x_i$. The **Perceptron Convergence Theorem** guarantees the algorithm finds a separating hyperplane in a finite number of updates *if and only if the data is linearly separable*; if not separable, it never converges (keeps oscillating) — this is the key structural difference from logistic regression, which always converges to a (possibly "soft"/non-separating) solution because it optimizes a smooth loss rather than looking for a hard separator.

**Multi-class extensions.** **One-vs-rest** trains one binary classifier per class (class $k$ vs. all others) and picks the class with the highest score; **one-vs-one** trains a classifier per pair of classes and takes a majority vote — one-vs-rest needs $K$ classifiers, one-vs-one needs $\binom{K}{2}$, trading fewer/larger training problems against more/smaller ones. **Softmax regression** generalizes logistic regression directly to $K$ classes with a single joint model.

**Exam angle.** Derive/trace a perceptron weight update on a small labeled example. Explain why perceptron doesn't converge on non-separable data while logistic regression still produces a (probabilistic) answer. Compare decision boundaries and loss surfaces (0/1-loss-like hard update vs. smooth cross-entropy) between the two.

title: 05 — Learning Theory (VC / PAC)
body:
**PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) learning** formalizes "how much data do I need to learn well." A hypothesis class is PAC-learnable if, with probability at least $1-\delta$, we can find a hypothesis with true error at most $\epsilon$, given a training-set size that grows polynomially in $1/\epsilon$, $1/\delta$, and the complexity of the hypothesis class. The bound formalizes the intuition that more data reduces error and increases confidence, but the required amount also grows with how expressive/large the hypothesis class is.

**VC dimension** measures the capacity/expressiveness of a hypothesis class: it's the size of the largest set of points that the class can **shatter** (label in every possible way, i.e. realize all $2^n$ labelings). E.g. a linear classifier in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has VC dimension 3 (can shatter any 3 non-collinear points, but not 4 points in general position). Higher VC dimension → more expressive class → can fit more/harder patterns but needs proportionally more data to generalize (higher variance risk) — VC dimension is the theoretical counterpart of "model complexity" from the bias-variance discussion.

**Generalization bound (sketch).** True error $\le$ training error $+ O\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{VC\dim \cdot \log(n) }{n}}\right)$ (up to constants and a confidence term) — as the VC dimension grows relative to the sample size $n$, the gap between training and true error grows, which is the theoretical justification for regularization/model-selection controlling complexity relative to how much data you have.

**Exam angle.** Compute the VC dimension of a simple hypothesis class (intervals on a line, linear separators in 2D, axis-aligned rectangles) by exhibiting a shattered set of the claimed size and arguing no larger set can be shattered. Connect a higher VC dimension to a looser generalization guarantee for a fixed sample size.

title: Exercise-session slides — Multi-Armed Bandits
body:
**Setting.** At each round you pick one of $K$ arms (actions), observe a stochastic reward for the chosen arm only (not the others — no supervision on unchosen arms, unlike full-feedback online learning), and want to maximize cumulative reward, or equivalently minimize **regret**: the gap between your cumulative reward and that of always pulling the single best arm in hindsight.

**Exploration vs. exploitation.** The core tension: exploiting the arm that currently looks best forgoes information about other arms that might actually be better; exploring pulls suboptimal-looking arms to learn more, at the cost of reward now.

**Core algorithms:**
- **$\epsilon$-greedy** — with probability $\epsilon$ pick a random arm (explore), otherwise pick the current empirical best (exploit). Simple, but $\epsilon$ is typically fixed/decayed by a schedule rather than principled, and it keeps exploring uniformly even once an arm is clearly best.
- **UCB (Upper Confidence Bound)** — pick the arm maximizing $\hat{\mu}_a + \sqrt{\dfrac{2\log t}{n_a}}$: the empirical mean reward plus a confidence bonus that shrinks as an arm is pulled more ($n_a \uparrow$) and grows over time for rarely-pulled arms ($t \uparrow$) — this is "optimism in the face of uncertainty," and it achieves logarithmic regret (provably near-optimal) without needing a tunable exploration probability.
- **Thompson Sampling** — maintain a Bayesian posterior over each arm's reward distribution, sample one value per arm from its posterior each round, and pull the arm with the highest sample; naturally balances exploration (wide posteriors get sampled optimistically sometimes) and exploitation (narrow, high-mean posteriors dominate) without an explicit bonus term.

**Exam angle.** Trace $\hat\mu_a$ and the UCB bonus over a few rounds of a small numeric example to determine which arm gets pulled next. Explain qualitatively why UCB's bonus term encodes exploration (shrinks with more pulls, i.e. more confidence) and contrast it with $\epsilon$-greedy's non-adaptive exploration.

title: 08 — Markov Decision Processes (MDP)
body:
**MDP formalism.** An RL problem is a Markov Decision Process: states $S$, actions $A$, transition probabilities $P(s'|s,a)$, reward function $R(s,a,s')$, discount factor $\gamma \in [0,1)$. The **Markov property** means the next state/reward depends only on the current state and action, not the full history. A **policy** $\pi(a|s)$ maps states to (distributions over) actions; the goal is to find $\pi^*$ maximizing expected discounted cumulative reward $\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_t \gamma^t r_t\right]$.

**Value functions.** $V^\pi(s)$ = expected return starting from state $s$ and following $\pi$; $Q^\pi(s,a)$ = expected return starting from $s$, taking action $a$, then following $\pi$. Both satisfy the **Bellman equation** — a recursive decomposition of value into immediate reward plus discounted value of the next state, e.g. $Q^\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s'} P(s'|s,a)\big[R(s,a,s') + \gamma \sum_{a'}\pi(a'|s')Q^\pi(s',a')\big]$ — this recursion is what every RL algorithm in the next two sections exploits to learn values without evaluating full trajectories.

**Exam angle.** Given a small MDP (states, transitions, rewards), write out the Bellman equation for a specific state-action pair and evaluate it numerically given known/assumed value estimates. Explain what the Markov property buys computationally (constant-size backup per state, independent of history length).

**$\gamma$'s role.** $\gamma$ close to 0 makes the agent myopic (only immediate reward matters); $\gamma$ close to 1 makes it far-sighted (future rewards matter almost as much as immediate ones) — be ready to reason about how a decision changes as $\gamma$ varies.

title: 09 — Solving MDPs
body:
**Setting.** Given a *known* MDP model (transition probabilities $P$ and rewards $R$ are known upfront), find the optimal policy $\pi^*$ by solving the Bellman **optimality** equations directly — no interaction/exploration with an environment is needed yet (that comes with the model-free methods in Reinforcement Learning).

**Value iteration.** Repeatedly apply the Bellman optimality backup to every state, $V(s) \leftarrow \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s'|s,a)[R(s,a,s') + \gamma V(s')]$, until $V$ converges (changes fall below a threshold); the optimal policy is then read off greedily from the converged $V$. Guaranteed to converge because the Bellman optimality operator is a contraction (with factor $\gamma$) under the discounted setting.

**Policy iteration.** Alternates two steps until the policy stops changing: **policy evaluation** (compute $V^\pi$ exactly for the current policy $\pi$, by solving the linear system given by the Bellman equation for $\pi$, or by iterating it) and **policy improvement** (update $\pi(s) \leftarrow \arg\max_a \sum_{s'} P(s'|s,a)[R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^\pi(s')]$ for every state). Typically converges in fewer iterations than value iteration (each iteration does more work — a full policy evaluation — but needs fewer outer iterations to reach the optimal policy).

**Exam angle.** Trace one or two iterations of value iteration or policy iteration by hand on a small grid-world-style MDP, updating $V$ (or $\pi$) state by state. Explain the practical trade-off between the two (value iteration: cheap per-iteration, more iterations; policy iteration: expensive per-iteration policy evaluation, fewer iterations) and why both require the model ($P$, $R$) to be known, unlike Q-learning/SARSA.

title: 10 — Reinforcement Learning
body:
**Model-free setting.** Unlike value/policy iteration, here $P$ and $R$ are **unknown** — the agent only learns from experienced transitions/rewards by interacting with the environment, which reintroduces the **exploration vs. exploitation** tension seen in Multi-Armed Bandits (bandits are the special case of RL with a single state).

**Q-learning** — model-free, off-policy: update $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\big[r + \gamma\max_{a'}Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)\big]$ using the *max* over next actions regardless of what action was actually taken next — this is why it's **off-policy** (it learns the optimal $Q$ even while following an exploratory, non-optimal behavior policy, e.g. $\epsilon$-greedy).

**SARSA** — model-free, on-policy: same update but uses the action *actually taken* next, $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\big[r + \gamma Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)\big]$, so it learns the value of the policy it's actually following (including its exploration), making it **on-policy**.

**Exam angle.** Trace a Q-learning or SARSA update numerically for one or two steps given a small MDP/reward and a chosen action sequence. Explain the on-policy/off-policy distinction concretely in terms of which action's $Q$-value bootstraps the update (max over actions vs. the actually-taken next action), and connect it back to why value/policy iteration don't need this distinction (they have the model, so there's no "behavior policy" doing exploration).

title: Model Evaluation & Cross-Validation
body:
**Classification metrics.** From the confusion matrix (TP, FP, TN, FN): **Accuracy** $=\frac{TP+TN}{TP+FP+TN+FN}$ is misleading under **class imbalance** (a classifier that always predicts the majority class can score high accuracy while being useless). **Precision** $=\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ (of predicted positives, how many are truly positive — matters when false positives are costly). **Recall/Sensitivity** $=\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ (of actual positives, how many are found — matters when false negatives are costly). **F1-score** $=2\cdot\frac{\text{Precision}\cdot\text{Recall}}{\text{Precision}+\text{Recall}}$, the harmonic mean, balancing both. The **ROC curve** plots True Positive Rate vs. False Positive Rate as the classification threshold varies; **AUC** (area under the ROC curve) summarizes performance across all thresholds, threshold-independent, and is more informative than accuracy on imbalanced datasets.

**Cross-validation.** **k-fold CV** splits the training data into $k$ equal folds, trains on $k-1$ folds and validates on the remaining fold, rotating which fold is held out, and averages the $k$ validation scores — this gives a more stable estimate of generalization performance than a single train/validation split, at the cost of $k\times$ the training time. **Leave-one-out CV** is the extreme case $k=n$: nearly unbiased but very expensive and high-variance across folds for large $n$. **Stratified k-fold** preserves class proportions in every fold — important with imbalanced classes so no fold accidentally lacks the minority class.

**Correct procedure.** Cross-validation is used to *select* hyperparameters/models (never to report a final unbiased performance number) — after selecting the best configuration by CV, retrain on the full training set and evaluate once, and only once, on a separate held-out test set that played no role in selection.

**Exam angle.** Given a confusion matrix, compute precision/recall/F1/accuracy and reason about which metric fits a stated scenario (e.g. medical screening cares about recall/false negatives; spam filtering cares about precision/false positives). Explain why k-fold CV gives a lower-variance estimate than a single split, and why using the test set for hyperparameter tuning invalidates the final performance report.
